title: Inflation surge to ‘rubber stamp’ fresh wave of interest rate hikes
full_text: Other Editions 29 April 2026 Business Inflation surge to ‘rubber stamp’ fresh wave of interest rate hikes theaustralian.com.au Duncan Evans 28 April 2026 06:16 GMT 810 words English AUSTOL © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. Australia’s inflation rate is expected to jump to 4.5 per cent and could crash through the 5 per cent barrier in Wednesday’s ‘crucial’ CPI data release, triggering a fresh wave of interest rate rises, economists warn. In the first indication of how the Iran War oil shock may affect Australia’s already sticky inflation problem, economists were split on how much prices would rocket, but all agreed it would be bad news for Aussies already struggling with cost-of-living pressures. The stark acceleration - if it materialises - would immediately heap pressure on the Reserve Bank of Australia to lift interest rates to tame escalating price pressures, with a new hike to flow through as early as next week. “It’s going to be a shock,” IG markets analyst Tony Sycamore told News Corp. “Households are already dealing with cost of living pressures.” Economists predicted the year-over-year headline inflation number, which includes price changes across all categories including fuel, food and electricity, to lift from 3.7 per cent recorded last month to more than four per cent. Investment banking giant Goldman Sachs expects the figure to hit 5.1 per cent, while Barrenjoey is predicting 4.6 per cent. Banking giant Westpac expects an annual lift of 4.2 per cent. The trimmed quarterly measurement, which strips out volatile items like fuel, is also expected to lift from 3.3 per cent to around 3.5 per cent, with top end estimates at 3.6 per cent - all outside the RBA’s target inflation range of 2-3 per cent. The Iran War oil shock is behind the stark acceleration in prices, Westpac said in a note from Tuesday, and the price pain is likely to tighten over the year. “The Middle East conflict is the largest energy shock since the oil crises of the 1970s and 80s,” the note read. “It started February 28 so its impact was only felt through March, mostly via autofuel. As such we expect a much broader impact in the June quarter and through the second half of the year. “Core inflation (the trimmed measurement) is not immune to the Middle East crisis. While the impact is likely to be more significant through the second half of the year, we are expecting a 0.93 per cent increase in the March quarter – matching the 0.9 per cent December quarter gain.” EQ Economics Managing Director Warren Hogan says the RBA’s handling of inflation has “not worked” to get it under control. Mr Sycamore said Wednesday’s “crucial release” would almost certainly push the RBA to lift rates when it meets again next week. “It’s going to take an extremely soft number to prevent a rate hike, but that looks unlikely given what we’re seeing out there with regards to petrol prices,” he said. “If it doesn’t show some significant size of being weaker than expected, then it does rubber stamp that rate hike next week.” The markets now expect a further two or three hikes this year, which would cancel out the rate cuts the RBA delivered in 2025 and push the cash rate back to around 4.75 per cent. At the moment, the cash rate is pegged at 4.1 per cent. “An RBA rate hike next week is viewed as more likely than not, with swaps markets pricing it in at 70 per cent,” Westpac economist Ryan Wells said. “Another hike beyond that is pencilled in later in the year, but this week’s inflation data will play a key role in finetuning these expectations ahead of next week’s decision.” Australia’s inflation landscape will be largely determined by how long the Strait of Hormuz shipping lane remains shut, Mr Sycamore added, warning there were no clear signs of a swift resolution. “It looks like he (President Trump) is digging his heels in and it’s going to be, the ball is in Iran’s court now,” he said. “And even yesterday we saw some sense of de-escalation out there when Iran said, ‘we will open the Strait, but we want to talk about the nuclear side of things later’, and that’s just not what President Trump’s interested in.” Mr Sycamore said a big jump in headline inflation could feed into “inflation expectations”, or how regular people expect prices to change and modify their behaviour accordingly. “It’s the hit to confidence out there that ‘hey, interest rates are going to have to go higher because the line CPI is at 5 per cent’, and that feeds through into this inflationary expectations cycle which we’re currently seeing,” he said. The Australian Bureau of Statistics releases the CPI data at 11.30am AEST Wednesday. Nationwide News Pty Ltd. summary: Australia's inflation rate is projected to rise to 4.5% or higher, driven by the Iran War oil shock, prompting expectations of interest rate hikes from the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA). Economists predict that the Consumer Price Index (CPI) data release will likely lead to a rate increase as early as next week. The anticipated inflation surge could result in further rate hikes throughout the year, reversing previous cuts.
publication:
Australia lacks national strategy to regulate AI spread in workplace, report states
Australian Broadcasting Corporation News28 April 20261,245 wordsnational work reporter Bronwyn Herb

Australia risks repeating the mistakes it made with social media unless it moves quickly to regulate the spread of artificial intelligence in the workplace, according to a new report.
key_points: Inflation expected to rise to 4.5% or more, impacting cost of living.
RBA likely to increase interest rates in response to inflation data.
Further rate hikes anticipated later in the year, reversing 2025 cuts.
relevance: Understanding inflation trends and interest rate movements is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals in managing risk and advising clients.
